flashcards.front: What is a proposition in propositional logic?
flashcards.back: A proposition is a declarative statement that is either completely true or completely false, but not both. Examples: '2+2=4' (true), 'Delhi is the capital of India' (true), '5>7' (false).
A truth table is a mathematical table that shows all possible truth values of a compound proposition for every combination of truth values of its component propositions.

flashcards.front: Define logical negation (¬) and its truth table
flashcards.back: Negation (NOT) reverses the truth value. If p is true, ¬p is false; if p is false, ¬p is true. Truth table: T→F, F→T.

flashcards.front: Define logical conjunction (∧) and its truth table
flashcards.back: Conjunction (AND) is true only when both operands are true. Truth table: T∧T=T, T∧F=F, F∧T=F, F∧F=F.

flashcards.front: Define logical disjunction (∨) and its truth table
flashcards.back: Disjunction (OR) is false only when both operands are false. Truth table: T∨T=T, T∨F=T, F∨T=T, F∨F=F.

flashcards.front: Define implication (p→q) and its truth table
flashcards.back: Implication (p→q) is false only when p is true and q is false. Truth table: T→T=T, T→F=F, F→T=T, F→F=T. Also called 'if p then q'.

flashcards.front: Define biconditional (p↔q) and its truth table
flashcards.back: Biconditional (p↔q) is true when both operands have the same truth value. Truth table: T↔T=T, T↔F=F, F↔T=F, F↔F=T. Also called 'p if and only if q'.
A tautology is a compound proposition that is always true regardless of the truth values of its component propositions. Example: p∨¬p (Law of Excluded Middle).
A contradiction is a compound proposition that is always false regardless of the truth values of its component propositions. Example: p∧¬p.
A contingency is a compound proposition that is neither always true nor always false; its truth value depends on the truth values of its components.
Two propositions p and q are logically equivalent (p≡q) if they have identical truth values for all possible combinations of truth values of their components.

flashcards.front: State the Commutative Laws for ∧ and ∨
flashcards.back: p∧q ≡ q∧p and p∨q ≡ q∨p. These laws state that the order of operands does not affect the result for AND and OR operations.

flashcards.front: State the Associative Laws for ∧ and ∨
flashcards.back: (p∧q)∧r ≡ p∧(q∧r) and (p∨q)∨r ≡ p∨(q∨r). These laws state that grouping of operands does not affect the result for AND and OR operations.
¬(p∧q) ≡ ¬p∨¬q and ¬(p∨q) ≡ ¬p∧¬q. Negation of a conjunction is the disjunction of negations, and vice versa.
A compound proposition (or molecular statement) is formed by combining two or more propositions using logical connectives like ∧, ∨, →, ↔, and ¬.
